question: Explain the fundamental geometric idea behind the least squares solution to an inconsistent system Ax = b. Describe what the least squares solution minimizes and why this is a useful approach.
expectedPoints: The system Ax = b is inconsistent when b is not in the column space of A.
The goal is to find the vector x̂ such that A x̂ is the projection of b onto the column space of A, Col(A).
This minimizes the length of the residual vector, ||b - A x̂||, which represents the error.
It is useful because it finds the 'best possible' solution, even when a perfect solution doesn't exist, by minimizing the sum of the squared errors.

question: Derive the normal equations, AᵀA x̂ = Aᵀb, from the geometric principle of orthogonality. State the principle and show the algebraic steps that lead to this central formula.
expectedPoints: The geometric principle: The residual vector e = b - A x̂ must be orthogonal to the column space of A for x̂ to be the least squares solution.
This orthogonality condition is written as e ⟂ Col(A), meaning e is orthogonal to every column vector in A.
This is equivalent to Aᵀ(b - A x̂) = 0.
Rearranging this equation gives the normal equations: AᵀA x̂ = Aᵀb.

question: Given a simple data set and a model (e.g., fitting a line y = c₀ + c₁x to three points), set up the matrix equation Ax = b. Explain why this system is likely inconsistent and describe what the least squares error represents in this context.
expectedPoints: Construct the matrix A (the design matrix) and vector b from the given data points.
Explain that with more equations (points) than unknowns (c₀, c₁), a perfect fit is unlikely, making the system inconsistent.
The least squares error is the minimum value of ||b - A x̂||, which is the square root of the sum of the squared vertical distances between the data points and the fitted line.
It provides a quantitative measure of how well the model fits the data.
